#Summary1=(human1) This document contains a Montana Supreme Court case regarding a defendant’s appeal based on the fact that she was forced to attend her trial by Zoom for the jury to be able to assess her mental health, despite regulations allowing defendants to decline participation via Zoom.
#Text=All rise and give your attention to --
#Text=Morning , everyone .
#Text=This is the time set for the oral argument in cause number DA22-0645 in the matter of the mental health of T. W.
#Text=And I understand , uh , Miss Peterson , you 're going to reserve ten minutes for your rebuttal , is that correct ?
#Text=That is correct .
#Text=Okay .
#Text=And Justice Gustafason , you can hear and see us okay ?
#Text=Yes .
#Text=Okay .
#Text=Ready to begin , counsel .
#Text=Good morning and may it please the court .
#Text=My name is Kristin Peterson , and I am pleased to represent appellant and respondent T. W.

#Text=T. W. comes today asking this court to preserve the integrity of the jury system and Montana laws ' respect for those the state seeks to institutionalize for forced mental health treatment as a last resort .
#Text=Miss Peterson , can you pull the mic a little closer to you ?
#Text=Is that better ?

#Text=The state can not compel a respondent who knowingly wishes to waive their appearance at trial , to be spotlighted by Zoom against their will for the express reasons , so the jury may inspect them for mental defects .

#Text=And a jury 's verdict can not be reduced to a mere dress rehearsal for a second successive petition and second jury trial within days of the first , where the state takes another bite at the apple on the same evidence , the prior jury rejected to prove the same theory as to the same person 's need for commitment , for being unable to care for her basic needs .
#Text=This case presents two issues for this court , either of which is grounds for reversal of T. W. 's involuntary commitment to Montana State Hospital .

#Text=First , the district court turned Montana law on its head and unlawfully forced T. W. 's remote appearance at trial by Zoom through an unmuted and video on connection , despite T. W. 's request to waive her appearance .

#Text=Second , the district court incorrectly ruled that claim or issue preclusion categorically had no impact on either A. , the state 's ability to proceed on its second successive petition , or B. , do so by use of the same exact evidence presented at the previous jury trial that had ended with dismissal on the merits , on that jury 's finding that T. W. did not require commitment .
#Text=We will start today by addressing T. W. 's compelled remote appearance .

#Text=As we know , this case involves back to back commitment petitions and back to back jury trials on those petitions within days of each other after the first jury found T. W. did not require commitment .

#Text=At T. W. 's second jury trial , she made the same request that was allowed at her prior trial to waive her appearance and watch trial by Zoom from a separate room where the video and audio were off .

#Text=Although the state did not previously object this time , the state objected and argued T. W. should either be in the courtroom or audible and visible by Zoom expressly so the jury would have the opportunity to observe her mental condition .
#Text=The district court agreed , reversed course and determined the jury was entitled to inspect T. W. for mental defects .
#Text=The court allowed T. W. to go to a separate room and watch by Zoom , but required her video and audio to remain on to facilitate her personal inspection by the jury .
#Text=The district court persisted in that order , despite T. W. 's objection and her unequivocal request to not use Zoom at all .

#Text=The district court 's ruling forcing T. W. to remain on Zoom violated Montana 's civil commitment scheme and the rights it granted T. W. to be present and participate in the proceeding , and waive that right if she so desired , and was capable of doing so under 5321 119 , and 5321 140 , which she undisputedly was .
#Text=Montana 's involuntary commitment scheme is part of a rather recent development in the law to establish strong procedural safeguards for those facing involuntary commitment .
#Text=The scheme is to be administered with full respect for the person 's dignity and personal integrity , as well as to ensure that due process of law .
#Text=As part of the scheme , the respondent has the right to be present and participate in a proceeding .

#Text=5321 119 expressly provides a capable respondent may waive their right to be present and participate , and this court has consistently interpreted 119 ( 1 ) to only confer discretion on the court to ensure the waiver is knowing and voluntary .

#Text=5321 140 piggybacks 119 on a person 's presence by Zoom , making it subject to the waiver provisions of 119 and providing in subsection five , the respondent can choose not to appear by Zoom at all .
#Text=Miss Peterson , can I ask you on section 119 , do we have -- we we do n't have any case law that has interpreted the facts that we have here .
#Text=We do n't -- we have n't seen that lax scenario and the implication of 119 .
#Text=That is true , Your Honor .

#Text=This court has consistently interpreted it in such a way that , um , that the court 's only discretion is to ensure that the waiver is knowing and voluntary and relevant to this in this regard is this court 's decision in in re PAC , a 2013 decision , in which case the respondent 's attorney represented that the respondent did n't wish to be present , and the court reversed that because there was not a sufficient record that the waiver was knowing and voluntary , but made sure to state very clearly that obviously there is no reason to require a respondent like PAC to attend the hearing before her attorney can inform the court that she wishes to waive her right to be present .
#Text=Such a requirement would elevate form over substance , and would be a disservice to the respondent who does not wish to attend .
